task: I’m trying to put together a really cheap late-November city-break from London and want a realistic shortlist I can actually look at in the browser. Please start on Skyscanner and search round-trip flights leaving from London for November 18 and coming back November 28 then pull out four destination options that look viable and note the destination city or airport and the lowest price shown for each so I can see which places are even in budget. Open the most promising flight results in their own tabs and leave the cheapest-looking Skyscanner option visible so I have a reference point. Once you’ve got that shortlist, use it to decide which destination appears cheapest overall. After that, go to Ryanair and check London to Budapest for the week starting November 18 because Budapest is usually a low-cost fallback for me and I want to know whether it still deserves a spot on the list; list the cheapest available options you can find in ascending price order and keep the Budapest results page open so I can compare it visually with Skyscanner. Since I may need to stay in London the night before flying, switch to Booking.com and look up 22 Suites in London, report its guest review score, and summarize at least three recent guest reviews so I can tell whether the nice rating actually matches what people are saying. Then do the same quality check for The Chapter Hotels – Finsbury Park by reporting its overall review score and review descriptor and reading into the recent reviews enough to judge whether it really seems dependable for a one-night pre-flight stay. If possible, open both hotel pages in separate tabs and leave the review sections visible so I can compare them side by side. In the end, give me a short recommendation on which flight option you’d prioritize, whether Budapest should stay on the shortlist as a backup, and which of those two London hotels seems more reliable for the night before an early flight.
grader_reasoning:
R1: success - The agent successfully navigated Skyscanner, performed an "Everywhere" search for the specified dates (Nov 18 to Nov 28), and extracted four destination options (Wroclaw, Gdansk, Katowice, and Krakow) along with their lowest displayed prices (£32, £36, £44, and £58 respectively). This information was clearly presented in the final summary.

R2: success - The agent successfully searched Skyscanner for flights from London to "Everywhere" for the specified dates. It identified four destination options (Wroclaw, Gdansk, Katowice, and Krakow) and correctly determined that Wroclaw was the cheapest at £32. It opened the relevant Skyscanner results pages in separate tabs and maintained them throughout the process, satisfying the requirement to identify the cheapest option and keep it visible/open.

R3: success - The agent successfully navigated to the Ryanair website, searched for London to Budapest flights for the week starting November 18, and retrieved the available flight options. In its final response (step 76), it explicitly listed the cheapest available options in ascending price order: £37.99 (Nov 18), £37.99 (Nov 24), £52.99 (Nov 21/23), £58.34 (Nov 19), and £60.99 (Nov 20/22). This confirms the requirement to list the cheapest options in ascending order.

R4: success - The agent successfully navigated to the Booking.com page for "15 min to Central London Guest Suites 22" (which is the property identified as 22 Suites). It opened the review section, identified the guest review score as 7.1, and provided a summary of recent guest reviews (mentioning cleanliness, comfort, and transport links as positives, and safety, dirty common areas, and plumbing issues as negatives). The verification requirements are met.

R5: success - The agent successfully navigated to the Booking.com page for "The Finsbury Park Hotel" (which it correctly identified as the property requested). In step 74, the agent opened the review modal, which explicitly displays "Scored 7.9 Rated good". The agent then summarized the reviews in step 76, noting that recent reviews are generally positive ("Several recent 8-10 reviews praise cleanliness, quietness, location, and value"), which is supported by the high score and the context of the review modal. The requirement to report the score, descriptor, and judge the positivity of recent reviews is satisfied.

R6: success - The agent successfully searched Skyscanner for round-trip flights from London to various destinations, identifying Wroclaw (£32), Gdansk (£36), Katowice (£44), and Krakow (£58) as the shortlist. It then searched Ryanair for London to Budapest, finding one-way fares starting at £37.99. In its final recommendation (Step 76), the agent explicitly states: "Budapest should stay as a backup, but it does not beat the cheapest Skyscanner round-trip options on this pass," thereby comparing the Ryanair pricing against the Skyscanner shortlist as required by the rubric.

R7: success - The agent successfully completed all parts of the user task. It searched Skyscanner for flights from London for the specified dates, identified four destination options (Wroclaw, Gdansk, Katowice, Krakow) with their lowest prices, and checked Ryanair for London to Budapest flights. It also performed the requested quality check on two London hotels (22 Suites and The Chapter Hotels – Finsbury Park) using Booking.com reviews. Finally, it provided a clear recommendation prioritizing the cheapest flight (Wroclaw), evaluating Budapest as a backup, and comparing the reliability of the two hotels based on the review evidence gathered.
